problems.title: Multiples of 3 or 5
problems.description:
Given a limit n, find the sum of all natural numbers below n that are divisible by 3 or 5.

This is the classic warmup problem and a good first AlterN challenge.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 1000000
Use 64-bit arithmetic for the result.
problems.outputFormat: Print the sum of all natural numbers below n divisible by 3 or 5.
problems.hintContent: A direct loop passes, but there is also a pattern: multiples of 3 and 5 form arithmetic progressions.
problems.editorialContent: Sum the multiples of 3, sum the multiples of 5, then subtract the multiples of 15. Each partial sum is an arithmetic series.

examples.explanation: This is the classic reference sample.

problems.title: Even Fibonacci Sum
problems.description:
Sum every even Fibonacci number that is strictly smaller than n.

The sequence begins with 1, 2, 3, 5, 8 and so on.
problems.constraints:
3 <= n <= 4000000
Use 64-bit arithmetic for the result.
problems.outputFormat: Print the sum of all even Fibonacci terms smaller than n.
problems.hintContent: Generate Fibonacci numbers in order and notice that every third term is even.
problems.editorialContent: A simple loop over Fibonacci values is enough, but the stronger observation is that the even subsequence can be generated directly.

examples.explanation: The even terms below 100 are 2, 8 and 34.

problems.title: Sum Square Difference
problems.description: For the first n natural numbers, compute the square of the sum minus the sum of the squares.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 100000
The answer may exceed 32-bit integer range.
problems.hintContent: This problem becomes much cleaner once you write both pieces with closed-form sums.
problems.editorialContent: Use n(n + 1) / 2 for the first sum and n(n + 1)(2n + 1) / 6 for the sum of squares, then subtract.

problems.title: Largest Prime Factor
problems.description:
Given a positive integer n, find its largest prime factor.

The key is to shrink n while you search.
problems.constraints:
2 <= n <= 600851475143
Use 64-bit arithmetic.
problems.outputFormat: Print the largest prime factor of n.
problems.hintContent: Whenever you find a factor, divide it out completely before moving on.
problems.editorialContent: Repeatedly divide n by the current factor. Once factor * factor exceeds the remaining n, any leftover value greater than 1 is the answer.

problems.title: Smallest Multiple
problems.description: For a given n, find the smallest positive number that is evenly divisible by every integer from 1 to n.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 20
The answer fits in 64-bit signed integer range for this catalog.
problems.outputFormat: Print the least common multiple of 1, 2, ..., n.
problems.hintContent: You do not need to test every candidate number. Build the answer from gcd/lcm relationships.
problems.editorialContent: Start with 1 and iteratively combine the next integer using lcm(a, b) = a / gcd(a, b) * b.

examples.explanation: 2520 is divisible by every integer from 1 to 10.
Reference value for the full range.

problems.title: Sum of Primes Below N
problems.description: Compute the sum of all prime numbers strictly smaller than n.
problems.constraints:
2 <= n <= 2000000
The answer fits in 64-bit signed integer range.
problems.outputFormat: Print the sum of all primes smaller than n.
problems.hintContent: Checking every number with trial division works for small limits, but a sieve scales much better.
problems.editorialContent: Run the Sieve of Eratosthenes up to n - 1, then sum the indices still marked prime.

problems.title: Largest Palindrome Product
problems.description: Given a digit count n, find the largest palindrome made from the product of two n-digit numbers.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 4
The catalog keeps n small enough for straightforward search with pruning.
problems.outputFormat: Print the largest palindrome that can be written as a product of two n-digit numbers.
problems.hintContent: If you iterate downward, you can stop inner loops once the product drops below the best result so far.
problems.editorialContent: Enumerate products in descending order and update the best palindrome found. Descending loops make pruning simple.

problems.title: Special Pythagorean Triplet
problems.description: Given a target sum n, find the product abc of a Pythagorean triplet a < b < c such that a + b + c = n. If no such triplet exists, return -1.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 5000
Return -1 when no valid triplet exists.
problems.outputFormat: Print the product abc, or -1 if there is no valid triplet.
problems.hintContent: Once you choose a and b, c is already fixed by n - a - b.
problems.editorialContent: Loop over a and b, derive c from the target sum, then check a^2 + b^2 = c^2.

problems.title: Spiral Diagonal Sum
problems.description: Start with 1 and build an odd-sized square spiral by moving to the right in a clockwise direction. Compute the sum of the numbers on both diagonals.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 1001
n is always odd.
problems.inputFormat: A single odd integer n, the side length of the spiral.
problems.outputFormat: Print the sum of the two diagonals in the n x n spiral.
problems.hintContent: Each square layer contributes exactly four new corner values. The step size is constant within that layer.
problems.editorialContent: Starting from 1, each ring adds four corners separated by the same step. Accumulate those corners layer by layer.

examples.explanation: The 5 x 5 spiral has diagonal sum 101.

problems.title: Amicable Numbers Sum
problems.description: For a given limit n, compute the sum of all amicable numbers smaller than n.
problems.constraints:
2 <= n <= 10000
Use proper divisors when building divisor sums.
problems.outputFormat: Print the sum of all amicable numbers smaller than n.
problems.hintContent: The expensive part is the sum of proper divisors. Build that value for each number first, then check pairs.
problems.editorialContent: Compute d(a) for every a below n, then count values where d(a) = b, d(b) = a and a != b.

examples.explanation: The pair 220 and 284 is the first amicable pair.

problems.title: Highly Divisible Triangle
problems.description: Find the first triangle number that has more than n divisors.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 200
Triangle numbers are 1, 3, 6, 10, 15, ...
problems.outputFormat: Print the first triangle number with strictly more than n divisors.
problems.hintContent: You do not need to enumerate every divisor. Prime factorization gives the divisor count much faster.
problems.editorialTitle: Grow triangle numbers and factor them
problems.editorialContent: Generate triangle numbers incrementally, factor each candidate, and convert the prime exponents into a divisor count.

examples.explanation: 28 has 6 divisors, which is more than 5.

problems.title: Lattice Paths
problems.description:
How many routes are there through an n x n grid if you can move only right or down?

This is a clean combinatorics problem disguised as path counting.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 20
Use 64-bit arithmetic for the answer.
problems.inputFormat: A single integer n, the side length of the square grid.
problems.outputFormat: Print the number of valid top-left to bottom-right paths.
problems.hintTitle: Every path is a sequence of moves
problems.hintContent: A valid path contains exactly n right moves and n down moves. Count how many distinct orderings are possible.
problems.editorialContent: Each route is an arrangement of n right moves among 2n total moves, so the answer is C(2n, n). Compute it carefully to avoid overflow in intermediate steps.

examples.explanation: There are 6 ways to arrange 2 right and 2 down moves.
Reference value for the standard 20 x 20 grid.

problems.title: Power Digit Sum
problems.description:
Given n, compute the sum of the decimal digits of 2^n.

The arithmetic is simple once you can represent the whole number correctly.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 1000
The full value of 2^n will not fit in standard integer types for larger n.
problems.outputFormat: Print the sum of the digits of 2 raised to the power n.
problems.hintContent: You only need the decimal digits of the power. A language big integer helps, but digit-by-digit multiplication also works.
problems.editorialTitle: Build the number, then sum digits
problems.editorialContent: Either use a built-in big integer type or store the decimal digits manually while doubling the number n times. Once 2^n is built, sum its digits.

problems.title: Factorial Digit Sum
problems.description: For a given n, compute n! and return the sum of its decimal digits.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 100
The factorial value grows very quickly, so plan for large integers or manual digits.
problems.outputFormat: Print the sum of the digits of n factorial.
problems.hintTitle: The hard part is storing n!
problems.hintContent: The answer is easy once the factorial is available. Think about how to represent very large decimal values.
problems.editorialContent: Accumulate the factorial from 1 to n using a big integer or manual digit array, then sum the digits of the final decimal representation.

problems.title: Distinct Powers
problems.description: For a given n, count how many distinct values appear in the set a^b where 2 <= a <= n and 2 <= b <= n.
problems.constraints:
2 <= n <= 100
Different base-exponent pairs can still produce the same value.
problems.outputFormat: Print the number of distinct terms in the set of powers.
problems.hintContent: Generating every value is straightforward for this input range. The main trick is making sure equivalent powers are counted only once.
problems.editorialContent: Enumerate every pair (a, b), compute a^b, and place the result in a set. The final set size is the answer for this catalog range.

examples.explanation: The 16 raw terms collapse to 15 distinct values because 4^2 = 2^4.
Reference value for the full range.

problems.title: Coin Sums
problems.description: Using the UK coin set 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 and 200, count how many distinct ways there are to make exactly n pence.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 200
Order does not matter; only the multiset of coins matters.
problems.inputFormat: A single integer n, the target amount in pence.
problems.outputFormat: Print the number of distinct coin combinations that sum to n.
problems.hintContent: If you generate permutations of the same coin set, you will overcount badly. Think in terms of coin types and dynamic programming.
problems.editorialContent: Maintain dp[x] = number of ways to build amount x. For each coin, update dp from left to right so every combination is counted once.

problems.title: Self Powers Tail
problems.description:
Compute the sum 1^1 + 2^2 + ... + n^n and print its last 10 digits.

When the result has fewer than 10 digits, print it normally without left-padding zeros.
problems.constraints:
1 <= n <= 1000
Use modular arithmetic to avoid constructing huge values unnecessarily.
problems.outputFormat: Print the last 10 digits of the self-power sum as a normal integer.
problems.hintContent: If the problem asks only for the last 10 digits, keep every intermediate computation modulo 10^10.
problems.editorialContent: For each i from 1 to n, compute i^i modulo 10^10, add it to the running sum, and reduce modulo 10^10 again.

examples.explanation: The sum of self powers up to 10 ends with 405071317.

problems.title: Longest Collatz Start
problems.description:
For a given limit n, find the starting number smaller than n that produces the longest Collatz sequence.

If multiple starting values have the same maximum length, return the smallest one.
problems.constraints:
2 <= n <= 100000
The Collatz rules are: n -> n / 2 when n is even, otherwise n -> 3n + 1.
problems.outputFormat: Print the starting number smaller than n with the longest Collatz chain.
problems.hintContent: Recomputing every chain from scratch wastes work. Store lengths you have already solved and reuse them.
problems.editorialContent: Evaluate Collatz lengths with caching. Then sweep all starting numbers below n and keep the best chain length, breaking ties toward the smaller starting value.

examples.explanation: 9 produces the longest chain among starting values below 10.

problems.title: Maximum Path Sum I
problems.description: Given a triangle of integers, find the maximum total from top to bottom by moving to adjacent numbers on the next row.
problems.constraints:
1 <= rows <= 15
The first input line gives the number of rows, and row i contains exactly i integers.
problems.inputFormat: The first line contains r. The next r lines contain the triangle rows, with row i holding i space-separated integers.
problems.outputFormat: Print the largest achievable path sum from the top row to the bottom row.
problems.hintTitle: The best choice depends on the future
problems.hintContent: A greedy step is not enough. Work from the bottom of the triangle upward so each parent sees the best continuation below it.
problems.editorialContent: Start from the last row and fold upward: each cell becomes its value plus the larger of its two children. The top cell becomes the answer.

problems.title: Double-Base Palindrome Sum
problems.description: Find the sum of all positive integers smaller than n that are palindromic in both base 10 and base 2.
problems.constraints:
10 <= n <= 1000000
Do not allow leading zeros in either representation.
problems.outputFormat: Print the sum of all numbers below n that are palindromic in base 10 and base 2.
problems.hintContent: A decimal palindrome is not automatically a binary palindrome. Test both string representations.
problems.editorialContent: Loop through the positive integers below n, test whether the decimal and binary representations are both palindromes, and sum the matches.

examples.explanation: The qualifying numbers below 100 are 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 33 and 99.
Reference value for the full range.
